text: The surgeon suspends the uterus to create space in the surgical field, allowing for a clear examination of the extent of the diverticular disease.
The surgeon marks the anticipated proximal extent of the resection area on the colon and examines the surrounding adhesions.
The surgeon initiates a medial to lateral dissection approach, likely targeting the sigmoid colon for resection.
The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the left colic artery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, progressing through the mesentery to isolate and prepare the relevant anatomical structures for the colectomy.
The surgeon begins the lateral dissection after reaching a sufficient lateral extent, continuing the procedure from the medial approach.
The surgeon demonstrates a limited portion of the lateral dissection, approaching the medial dissection plane to connect the two areas.

The video shows the diseased segment of the bowel with thick adhesions to the pelvic sidewall, characteristic of severe diverticular disease. These adhesions cause a visible angulation of the bowel due to intrinsic stricture.
The surgeon demonstrates the tight adhesion at the rectosigmoid junction, which visually appears to cause a stricture narrowing in the bowel.
The surgeon prepares the colon by dissecting superior to the superior hemorrhoidal artery, preserving the left colic artery, and extending up to the previously marked proximal margin.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the rectosigmoid junction and administers ICG to assess perfusion at the margin. The stapler is then fired to divide the distal colon.
The camera is positioned in arm number two, located in the epigastrium, providing a view of the posterior fornix. The surgeon inserts an EEA sizer and performs a colpotomy.
The surgeon positions the surgical instruments to make an incision as high as possible near the cervix, avoiding proximity to the rectovaginal septum to facilitate easier closure.
The surgeon closes the colpotomy after removing the specimen, which was not placed in a bag due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon uses a 2-0, twelve-inch V-lock suture to secure the first layer of closure, focusing on obtaining firm bites through the mucosal and muscular wall. This technique addresses bleeding from the mucosal edges torn during specimen extraction and ensures the integrity of the closure.

The surgeon focuses on suturing the muscular wall and mucosa, avoiding the overlying peritoneum. The camera is repositioned from arm three to arm two to provide a clearer view of the full closure process.

The surgeon completes the closure of the vaginal cuff and proceeds with a side-to-side, end-to-end anastomosis, joining the two posterior staple lines. The camera remains positioned in the epigastrium, providing a view of the surgical field as the surgeon performs a collotomy and proctotomy, followed by sewing an inner layer and an outer layer. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used for visualization.

The surgeon demonstrates the perfusion of the inner lips of mucosa and serosa with ICG after the proctotomy. The ICG enhancement is visible, particularly on the rectal side, while caution is advised against opening too far laterally to avoid ischemia.
The surgeon begins the anastomosis by placing a second suture in the middle, sewing out to the right, and then over the top to tie the sutures together. The procedure involves vaginal extraction.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a rigid scope inserted through the anastomosis. The scope reveals no bubbles, indicating a widely patent connection.
